Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis

They handle buffer creation (byteArray, charArray), content comparison using buffers (contentEquals), data copying with buffered operations (copy, copyLarge), skipping data in chunks (skip), converting streams to byte arrays with buffer limits (toByteArray), and writing data in buffered chunks (writeChunked). All operations are optimized around the DEFAULT_BUFFER_SIZE constant.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "buffered I/O operations" domain. They encapsulate the complexity of efficiently reading, writing, and processing I/O streams using fixed-size buffers. The shared constants (DEFAULT_BUFFER_SIZE, EOF, SOFT_MAX_ARRAY_LENGTH) define the buffering strategy and limits. This represents a cohesive subsystem for buffer management in I/O operations.

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates buffer management and buffered I/O operations from other I/O utilities
- Code Reusability: Buffer allocation and chunked operations can be reused across different I/O scenarios
- Complexity Reduction: Removes low-level buffering logic from the main IOUtils class
- Testability: Buffer-specific operations can be tested independently with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate buffer management concerns. The Strategy pattern is implicitly used here, where different buffering strategies (default size, soft limits) are encapsulated. This extraction creates a specialized component with high cohesion (all methods deal with buffered operations) and low coupling (minimal dependencies beyond standard I/O classes).
